content.text: What It Is

Logging and telemetry standards define the required shape, content, and routing of observability data emitted by agentic AI systems. Unlike traditional web services where the main observability concern is latency and error rate, agentic AI systems introduce additional observability dimensions: token consumption per call, model selection and version, prompt template identity, agent state transitions, tool invocation outcomes, and estimated cost per operation. Standards ensure that this richer data is captured consistently, that sensitive fields are redacted before the log reaches storage, and that distributed traces connect LLM calls across multi-service agentic workflows.

The two pillars of these standards are structured logging and distributed tracing. Structured logging means every log record is a JSON object with a defined schema — not a free-form string. This makes log records queryable, aggregatable, and parseable by observability tooling without custom parsing rules per service. Distributed tracing uses the OpenTelemetry standard to propagate trace and span context across service boundaries, so that a single user-visible agent turn can be followed across every microservice, LLM API call, and tool invocation it triggers.
Why It Matters

An agentic AI system that logs unstructured strings is opaque in production. When an agent fails or produces incorrect output, diagnosing the root cause requires reconstructing which model was called, which prompt template was used, how many tokens were consumed, and what tool calls were made — all from log records that contain only a timestamp and a message string. Structured logging makes this reconstruction automatic: every log record already carries the fields the diagnosis requires.

Token telemetry matters for cost control. LLM API costs are token-denominated. Without per-call token logging aggregated by model, operation, and user session, cost anomalies are invisible until the monthly invoice arrives. A single runaway agent session that issues thousands of tool-use calls with large context windows can consume budget equivalent to thousands of normal sessions. Real-time token telemetry enables budget alerting that can interrupt pathological sessions before they become invoice surprises.

PII redaction in logs is a regulatory requirement in most jurisdictions. GDPR, CCPA, and HIPAA all prohibit storing personal data in systems that are not purpose-limited and properly secured. Log backends are typically retained for 30-90 days and indexed across multiple copies. User prompts often contain personal information: names, addresses, health conditions, financial details. The standard requires that prompt text is never logged verbatim. Instead, services log a prompt hash and the prompt template identifier, which allows correlation without storing the sensitive content.
Benefits
Faster incident diagnosis.

Structured log records with trace IDs and model identifiers reduce mean time to diagnosis for LLM API failures from hours to minutes. Engineers can query 'all ERROR-level llm_call events for trace_id X' instead of grepping unstructured text.
Cost attribution.
Token telemetry aggregated by operation and user session provides per-feature cost attribution. This data enables informed decisions about prompt optimisation, model selection, and caching strategy.
Cache effectiveness measurement.
The token_cache_read field in every llm_call log record allows teams to measure the hit rate of prompt caching and tune cache-eligible prefixes to maximise cost savings.
Compliance evidence.
Audit logs with PII-redacted content and user_id_hash fields demonstrate compliance with data minimisation requirements without sacrificing the ability to correlate events to a user session.
Capacity planning input.
Latency histograms and token-per-call distributions from structured log aggregation provide the empirical basis for rate limit planning and model capacity negotiations.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Logging and telemetry pipeline: Application emits JSON log through PII Redactor to Collector (OpenTelemetry agent) to Log Backend. Separately, OTel SDK traces and Token Telemetry flow to Cost Dashboard and Alerting.
attrs.title: Structured Logging and Telemetry Pipeline

content.text: The pipeline separates two data flows. The upper path handles log records: the application emits a structured JSON record, the PII redactor scrubs sensitive fields in-process before the record leaves the service boundary, the OpenTelemetry collector batches and forwards to the log backend. The lower path handles trace spans: the OpenTelemetry SDK instruments HTTP calls and database queries automatically; manual instrumentation adds LLM-specific attributes to each span. Token telemetry feeds a cost dashboard and an anomaly alerting system that fires when token consumption per session exceeds a configured threshold.
Structured Logger Implementation

The following implementation provides a structured logger with built-in PII redaction and LLM call telemetry. The logger uses Python contextvars to propagate trace and correlation IDs across async call chains without requiring explicit parameter passing. The llm_call method records all token usage fields and computes an estimated cost using per-model pricing rates.

import json
import logging
import re
import time
import uuid
from contextvars import ContextVar
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Any

_correlation_id: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("correlation_id", default="")
_trace_id: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("trace_id", default="")
_span_id: ContextVar[str] = ContextVar("span_id", default="")

PII_PATTERNS = [
    (re.compile(r"[A-Za-z0-9._%+\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\-]+\.[A-Za-z]{2,}"), "[EMAIL_REDACTED]"),
    (re.compile(r"\b(?:\d[ \-]?){13,16}\b"), "[CARD_REDACTED]"),
    (re.compile(r"\b\d{3}[-.]?\d{2}[-.]?\d{4}\b"), "[SSN_REDACTED]"),
    (re.compile(r"sk-[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}"), "[API_KEY_REDACTED]"),
    (re.compile(r"Bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9\-._~+/]+=*"), "[BEARER_REDACTED]"),
]

def redact_pii(text: str) -> str:
    for pattern, replacement in PII_PATTERNS:
        text = pattern.sub(replacement, text)
    return text

class StructuredLogger:
    def __init__(self, service_name: str, environment: str, version: str):
        self.service_name = service_name
        self.environment = environment
        self.version = version
        self._logger = logging.getLogger(service_name)

    def _base_record(self, level: str, message: str, extra: dict) -> dict:
        record = {
            "timestamp_utc": datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
            "level": level,
            "service_name": self.service_name,
            "environment": self.environment,
            "version": self.version,
            "trace_id": _trace_id.get(""),
            "span_id": _span_id.get(""),
            "correlation_id": _correlation_id.get(""),
            "message": redact_pii(message),
        }
        for k, v in extra.items():
            if isinstance(v, str):
                record[k] = redact_pii(v)
            else:
                record[k] = v
        return record

    def info(self, message: str, **extra):
        rec = self._base_record("INFO", message, extra)
        self._logger.info(json.dumps(rec))

    def warning(self, message: str, **extra):
        rec = self._base_record("WARNING", message, extra)
        self._logger.warning(json.dumps(rec))

    def error(self, message: str, **extra):
        rec = self._base_record("ERROR", message, extra)
        self._logger.error(json.dumps(rec))

    def llm_call(
        self,
        model: str,
        operation: str,
        latency_ms: float,
        token_input: int,
        token_output: int,
        token_cache_read: int = 0,
        token_cache_write: int = 0,
        error_type: str | None = None,
    ):
        cost_usd = self._estimate_cost(model, token_input, token_output, token_cache_read)
        rec = self._base_record("INFO", "llm_api_call", {
            "event_type": "llm_call",
            "model": model,
            "operation": operation,
            "latency_ms": round(latency_ms, 1),
            "token_input": token_input,
            "token_output": token_output,
            "token_cache_read": token_cache_read,
            "token_cache_write": token_cache_write,
            "estimated_cost_usd": cost_usd,
            "error_type": error_type,
        })
        self._logger.info(json.dumps(rec))

    def _estimate_cost(self, model: str, inp: int, out: int, cache_read: int) -> float:
        rates = {
            "claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022": (3.0, 15.0, 0.30),
            "claude-3-5-haiku-20241022": (0.80, 4.0, 0.08),
        }
        r = rates.get(model, (1.0, 5.0, 0.10))
        return round((inp * r[0] + out * r[1] + cache_read * r[2]) / 1_000_000, 6)

def set_correlation_context(correlation_id: str = "", trace_id: str = "", span_id: str = ""):
    _correlation_id.set(correlation_id or str(uuid.uuid4()))
    _trace_id.set(trace_id)
    _span_id.set(span_id)
Required Log Fields
Universal Fields (every log record)
timestamp_utc.
ISO 8601 with microsecond precision and explicit UTC offset. Example: 2025-03-15T14:22:01.123456+00:00. Do not use Unix epoch integers — they are not human-readable in log viewers.
level.

One of DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL. Use the level policy defined in the standard: INFO for normal operations, WARNING for recoverable anomalies, ERROR for failed operations, CRITICAL for service-level failures.
trace_id and span_id.
OpenTelemetry trace and span IDs, formatted as 32-hex and 16-hex strings respectively. Empty string if no active trace context exists. These fields connect the log record to the distributed trace.
correlation_id.

A business-level identifier that is generated at the entry point of a user request and propagated across all downstream calls. Unlike trace_id which is infrastructure-scoped, correlation_id is business-scoped and persists across retry boundaries.
service_name.
The unique logical identifier of the emitting service, matching the service.name attribute in the OpenTelemetry resource. Used by the log backend to route records to service-specific dashboards.
LLM Call Fields (llm_call events)
model.
The exact model identifier as sent in the API request, e.g. claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022. Do not normalise or shorten — the exact identifier is required to compute cost using the current pricing table.
token_input.
The prompt token count as reported by the provider in the API response. Do not estimate from character count — provider tokenisation differs from simple character counting.
token_output.
The completion token count. This is the primary cost driver for most LLM operations and the most important field for cost anomaly detection.
token_cache_read.
Cache hit token count reported by the provider. This represents tokens that did not need to be processed by the model, reducing cost. Track this field to measure cache ROI.
latency_ms.
Wall-clock time from the moment the request was sent to the moment the last byte of the response was received. For streaming responses, this is the time to the final token.
Log Level Policy
DEBUG: Detailed diagnostic data — individual prompt tokens, intermediate reasoning steps, raw API responses. Never enabled in production. Only used in development and active debugging sessions.

INFO: Normal operational events. Every LLM API call, every tool invocation, every agent state transition, every user session start and end. These records form the primary data source for cost dashboards and capacity planning.

WARNING: Unexpected but recoverable conditions. Retry triggered (with attempt number and delay), cache miss on a hot path, fallback model selected because the primary model is unavailable, rate limit headroom dropping below 20%.

ERROR: Operation failures that require engineering investigation. Persistent API errors, DLQ enqueues, unhandled exceptions caught at the service boundary, tool call failures that abort the agent turn.

CRITICAL: Service-level failures that require immediate response. All retries exhausted with data loss, authentication or authorisation failures, security policy violations detected, circuit breaker open.
PII Redaction Strategy

Prompt text is the highest-risk field for PII exposure. Users routinely include personal information in their prompts without being aware that the text is being logged. The standard prohibits logging verbatim prompt text. Instead, log the prompt_hash (first 16 characters of the SHA-256 hash of the prompt, hex-encoded) and the prompt_template_id. This allows correlation of log records to a specific prompt without storing the content.

For fields other than prompt text, apply pattern-based redaction before emitting the log record. The PII pattern set must cover at minimum: email addresses, credit card numbers (Luhn-validatable 13-16 digit strings), social security numbers, API keys (provider-specific prefixes like sk-), and bearer tokens. Apply redaction in-process — do not rely on the log backend to perform redaction, because the record is already written to the network buffer before the backend processes it.
Never log.
Raw prompt text, raw completion text, API keys in request headers, bearer tokens, passwords, session cookies.
Always log with hash.
Prompt content (log prompt_hash instead), user messages (log user_id_hash instead of user identifier).
Safe to log.
Token counts, latency, model identifiers, operation names, error types, template IDs, prompt hashes.
Distributed Tracing with OpenTelemetry

OpenTelemetry (OTel) is the industry standard for distributed tracing. The OTel SDK instruments HTTP client libraries automatically, creating a span for every outbound HTTP call. For LLM API calls, the automatic HTTP instrumentation is a starting point, but it does not capture LLM-specific attributes. Manual instrumentation adds token counts, model identifiers, and operation names to the span, making the trace data actionable for AI system diagnosis.

from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.http.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource

def configure_tracing(service_name: str, environment: str, endpoint: str) -> trace.Tracer:
    resource = Resource.create({
        "service.name": service_name,
        "deployment.environment": environment,
    })
    provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)
    exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(endpoint=endpoint)
    provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
    trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
    return trace.get_tracer(service_name)

tracer = configure_tracing("agent-service", "prod", "http://collector:4318/v1/traces")

def traced_llm_call(model: str, prompt_hash: str, operation: str):
    with tracer.start_as_current_span(f"llm.{operation}") as span:
        span.set_attribute("llm.model", model)
        span.set_attribute("llm.operation", operation)
        span.set_attribute("llm.prompt_hash", prompt_hash)
        import time
        start = time.monotonic()
        result = {"input_tokens": 512, "output_tokens": 256, "cache_read": 128}
        latency_ms = (time.monotonic() - start) * 1000
        span.set_attribute("llm.token.input", result["input_tokens"])
        span.set_attribute("llm.token.output", result["output_tokens"])
        span.set_attribute("llm.token.cache_read", result["cache_read"])
        span.set_attribute("llm.latency_ms", round(latency_ms, 1))
        return result
YAML Logging and Telemetry Standard

# logging-standard.yaml
# Enterprise AI Engineering — Logging and Telemetry Standard v1.3

structured_logging:
  format: json
  required_fields:
    - timestamp_utc       # ISO 8601 with timezone, e.g. 2025-03-15T14:22:01.123456+00:00
    - level               # DEBUG | INFO | WARNING | ERROR | CRITICAL
    - service_name        # unique identifier for the emitting service
    - environment         # dev | staging | prod
    - version             # semver of the deployed service
    - trace_id            # OpenTelemetry trace ID (empty string if no active trace)
    - span_id             # OpenTelemetry span ID
    - correlation_id      # business-level ID propagated across service calls
    - message             # human-readable summary, PII-redacted
  conditional_fields:
    llm_calls:
      - model             # model identifier exactly as sent in the API request
      - operation         # logical operation name, e.g. "summarise_document"
      - latency_ms        # wall-clock time from request send to last byte received
      - token_input       # prompt token count
      - token_output      # completion token count
      - token_cache_read  # cache hit token count (provider-reported)
      - token_cache_write # cache write token count
      - estimated_cost_usd
    errors:
      - error_type        # transient | persistent | fatal
      - error_code        # machine-readable code, e.g. "RATE_LIMIT_EXCEEDED"
      - http_status_code
      - retry_attempt

log_levels:
  policy:
    DEBUG:
      use_for: >
        Detailed diagnostic information useful only during development or
        active debugging. Never enabled in production by default.
      production_enabled: false
    INFO:
      use_for: >
        Normal operational events: LLM calls, tool invocations, agent
        state transitions, user session start/end.
      production_enabled: true
    WARNING:
      use_for: >
        Unexpected conditions that do not prevent the operation from
        completing: retry triggered, cache miss, degraded mode active.
      production_enabled: true
    ERROR:
      use_for: >
        Operation failures that require investigation: persistent API
        errors, dead letter queue enqueues, unhandled exceptions.
      production_enabled: true
    CRITICAL:
      use_for: >
        Service-level failures: all retries exhausted with data loss,
        authentication failures, security policy violations.
      production_enabled: true

pii_redaction:
  apply_before_emit: true
  patterns:
    - name: email
      regex: "[A-Za-z0-9._%+\\-]+@[A-Za-z0-9.\\-]+\\.[A-Za-z]{2,}"
      replacement: "[EMAIL_REDACTED]"
    - name: api_key
      regex: "sk-[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}"
      replacement: "[API_KEY_REDACTED]"
    - name: bearer_token
      regex: "Bearer\\s+[A-Za-z0-9\\-._~+/]+="
      replacement: "[BEARER_REDACTED]"
  prompt_text:
    policy: never_log_verbatim
    alternatives:
      - log prompt_hash (SHA-256 truncated to 16 chars)
      - log prompt_length_chars and prompt_template_id

distributed_tracing:
  library: opentelemetry
  propagation_format: W3C_TraceContext
  auto_instrumented:
    - httpx
    - aiohttp
    - sqlalchemy
  manual_instrumentation_required:
    - llm_api_calls         # record token counts and model as span attributes
    - tool_invocations      # record tool_name and outcome as span attributes
    - agent_transitions     # record from_state and to_state

retention:
  hot_storage_days: 30
  warm_storage_days: 90
  cold_storage_days: 365
  compliance_hold_days: 2555  # 7 years for regulated industries
Key Takeaways
Every log record is a JSON object. Free-form strings are not acceptable in production systems. JSON makes records queryable without custom parsing.
Token usage fields (token_input, token_output, token_cache_read) must be logged on every LLM call. These fields are the primary input for cost dashboards and budget alerts.
PII redaction must be applied in-process before the record leaves the service boundary. Do not rely on log backends to redact — the data is already in the network buffer.
Prompt text is never logged verbatim. Log a SHA-256 hash (first 16 hex chars) and the template ID instead.
Correlation IDs propagate across service calls at the business level. Trace IDs propagate at the infrastructure level. Both are required for complete incident diagnosis.
OpenTelemetry spans for LLM calls must include token counts and model identifiers as span attributes. The automatic HTTP instrumentation is insufficient without these additions.
